article.title: Discoveries in structure and physiology of mechanically activated ion channels

references.unstructured: Anishkin, A., Loukin, S. H., Teng, J. & Kung, C. Feeling the hidden mechanical forces in lipid bilayer is an original sense. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. USA 111, 7898–7905 (2014).

references.author: B Coste
references.unstructured: Coste, B. et al. Piezo1 and Piezo2 are essential components of distinct mechanically activated cation channels. Science 330, 55–60 (2010). This study uses an RNA interference screen to identify PIEZOs as essential components of a mechanically actived ion channel.

references.author: SE Murthy
references.unstructured: Murthy, S. E., Dubin, A. E. & Patapoutian, A. Piezos thrive under pressure: mechanically activated ion channels in health and disease. Nat. Rev. Mol. Cell Biol. 18, 771–783 (2017).

references.author: SG Brohawn
references.unstructured: Brohawn, S. G., Su, Z. & MacKinnon, R. Mechanosensitivity is mediated directly by the lipid membrane in TRAAK and TREK1 K+ channels. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. USA 111, 3614–3619 (2014). This study demonstrates that the mechanically activated K2P channels, TRAAK and TREK1, are inherently mechanosensitive.

references.author: SE Murthy
references.unstructured: Murthy, S. E. et al. OSCA/TMEM63 are an evolutionarily conserved family of mechanically activated ion channels. eLife 7, 1–17 (2018). Several OSCA genes and their mammalian homologues, the TMEM63 family, are shown to be inherently mechanosensitive ion channels.

references.author: T Rasmussen
references.unstructured: Rasmussen, T., Flegler, V. J., Rasmussen, A. & Böttcher, B. Structure of the mechanosensitive channel MscS embedded in the membrane bilayer. J. Mol. Biol. 431, 3081–3090 (2019).

references.author: B Reddy
references.unstructured: Reddy, B., Bavi, N., Lu, A., Park, Y. & Perozo, E. Molecular basis of force-from-lipids gating in the mechanosensitive channel MscS. eLife 8, e50486 (2019). These two articles present cryo-EM structures of the bacterial MscS channel in a lipidic nanodisc, substantially updating our understanding of how it is embedded within the membrane.

references.author: P Jin
references.unstructured: Jin, P. et al. Electron cryo-microscopy structure of the mechanotransduction channel NOMPC. Nature 547, 118–122 (2017). This study presents the cryo-EM structure of NOMPC, revealing that the large ankyrin repeat domain is arranged with a large spring-like architecture.

references.author: Z Deng
references.unstructured: Deng, Z. et al. Cryo-EM and X-ray structures of TRPV4 reveal insight into ion permeation and gating mechanisms. Nat. Struct. Mol. Biol. 25, 252–260 (2018).

references.author: J Ge
references.unstructured: Ge, J. et al. Structure of mouse protocadherin 15 of the stereocilia tip link in complex with LHFPL5. eLife 7, e38770 (2018). Co-expression and purification of the MET complex components PCDH15 and LHFPL5 reveal that a TM helix of the PCDH15 subunit interacts extensively with the TM helices of each LHFPL5 subunit.

references.author: S Noreng
references.unstructured: Noreng, S., Bharadwaj, A., Posert, R., Yoshioka, C. & Baconguis, I. Structure of the human epithelial sodium channel by cryo-electron microscopy. eLife 7, e39340 (2018).

references.author: YR Guo
references.unstructured: Guo, Y. R. & MacKinnon, R. Structure-based membrane dome mechanism for Piezo mechanosensitivity. eLife 6, e33660 (2017).

references.author: K Saotome
references.unstructured: Saotome, K. et al. Structure of the mechanically activated ion channel Piezo1. Nature 554, 481–486 (2018).

references.author: Q Zhao
references.unstructured: Zhao, Q. et al. Structure and mechanogating mechanism of the Piezo1 channel. Nature 554, 487–492 (2018).

references.author: L Wang
references.unstructured: Wang, L. et al. Structure and mechanogating of the mammalian tactile channel PIEZO2. Nature 573, 225–229 (2019). These four studies present cryo-EM structures of PIEZO1 and PIEZO2, revealing that its curved shape probably resides within the membrane, and providing a near-atomic-resolution view of several features that may be involved in gating by mechanical force.

references.author: M Zhang
references.unstructured: Zhang, M. et al. Structure of the mechanosensitive OSCA channels. Nat. Struct. Mol. Biol. 25, 850–858 (2018).

references.author: S Jojoa-Cruz

references.author: K Maity
references.unstructured: Maity, K. et al. Cryo-EM structure of OSCA1.2 from Oryza sativa elucidates the mechanical basis of potential membrane hyperosmolality gating. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. USA 116, 14309–14318 (2019). These four studies present cryo-EM structures of OSCA1.2 and other OSCA family members, revealing their structural homology to TMEM16 and highlighting features that may be involved in mechanical gating.

references.unstructured: Bavi, N., Cox, C. D., Perozo, E. & Martinac, B. Toward a structural blueprint for bilayer-mediated channel mechanosensitivity. Channels 11, 91–93 (2017). This study proposed the dragging mechanism of mechanotransduction.
